docs.title: DynareJulia User Guide
docs.text: Welcome to DynareJulia User Guide. This guide is designed to help users get started, understand the workflow, and fully leverage the capabilities of DynareJulia.

docs.title: Acknowledgment
docs.text:
A huge thank you to Michel Juillard for his incredible work spanning more than two decades on the development of Dynare, and more recently, for his pioneering efforts on DynareJulia.

His contributions — from code and methodology to trainings and mentorship — have been instrumental in making these tools accessible to researchers and policymakers worldwide. His continuous support, generosity, and vision are deeply appreciated by the entire community.

docs.title: What is DynareJulia?
docs.text:
DynareJulia is a high-performance, modern, and flexible framework for solving, simulating, and analyzing dynamic economic models — written in Julia, and inspired by the powerful features of Dynare and the computational speed of Julia.

It is especially useful for researchers, economists, and policy analysts who need to:

  • Solve DSGE, OLG, and other structural models
  • Run simulations and perform sensitivity analysis
  • Customize model components using Julia's extensive scientific ecosystem

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• 🔗 Main GitHub Repository: DynareJulia/Dynare.jl Browse the codebase, contribute, or report issues.

  • 📘 Official Documentation: DynareJulia Documentation Explore API details, examples, and model syntax.

docs.text:

(For installation instructions, visit the Installation page.)

Happy modeling!

docs.title: ENDOCRED Model Simulations
docs.text: ENDOCRED (Endogenous Credibility) is a macroeconomic modeling framework that explicitly incorporates credibility dynamics into monetary policy decisions. It is widely used at central banks around the world, and in particulat, at the central banks that adopted prudent risk-management approach to monetary policy (FPAS MARK II) for forecasting and policy analysis.

docs.title: Brief Introduction to ENDOCRED
docs.text:
Why ENDOCRED?

  • Traditional FPAS models assume fixed policy credibility, often unrealistic during uncertain periods.
  • ENDOCRED addresses this by allowing public trust in the central bank to evolve endogenously over time.
  • It is especially relevant in contexts like post-pandemic inflation, where expectations, communication, and policy strength interact nonlinearly.

docs.title: Key Features of the ENDOCRED Framework
docs.text:
• Endogenous Credibility Process: Expectations react dynamically to how well the central bank achieves its objectives.
  • Two Inflation Regimes:
  • A credible regime (expectations anchored at 2%)
  • A high-inflation regime (persistent, drifting expectations)
  • Nonlinear Phillips Curve: The effect of output gaps on inflation intensifies in high-pressure economies.
  • Loss Function Approach: The model minimizes a central bank’s loss function instead of assuming a fixed interest rate rule.
  • DynareJulia Implementation: Fully implemented in open-source tools for fast policy simulation and transparency.

docs.title: Policy Simulations and Scenarios
docs.text:
The model supports scenario-based decision-making using cases such as:

  • Case A (Hawkish): Persistent inflation, requires strong policy tightening.
  • Case B (Dovish): Inflation is transitory, calls for mild adjustment.
  • Case X (Tail Risk): Stagflation or geopolitical shocks.

Alternative Paths for the Policy Rate

docs.title: Credibility Metrics
docs.text:
The framework introduces two empirical indicators:

  • CBSPII: Central Bank Sticky Price Inflation Indicator (e.g., based on Atlanta Fed sticky CPI)
  • CBCSI: Central Bank Credibility Stock Index

These help calibrate credibility loss and track its macroeconomic effects.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Credibility is a dynamic asset, not a fixed assumption.
  • Delays in tightening can lead to self-fulfilling inflation traps.
  • Models must account for expectation shifts in response to communication and action.
  • ENDOCRED helps to think about and model “dark corners” of monetary policy — such as stagflation, delayed disinflation, or excessive tightening.

📖 "In times of incredible uncertainty... a failure to act quickly, aggressively, and flexibly can lead to a very costly loss of credibility." — Kostanyan et al., 2022

docs.title: Simple Linear ENDOCRED
docs.text:
These are the model and script files:

  • simple_linear.mod
  • simple_linear.jl

docs.title: FPAS MARK II Scenarios - Model Codes in DynareJulia
docs.text:
These are the model and script files:

  • MARKET_2025Q1.mod
  • Case_A_2025Q1.mod
  • Case_B_2025Q1.mod
  • SCENARIOS_2025Q1.jl

Download them and place all four in the same project directory.

docs.title: ▶️ How to Run
docs.text:
1. Activate your project environment (or create one):
using Pkg
Pkg.activate("venv")  # or your preferred environment path

docs.title: Key References
docs.text:
The following academic and policy-oriented references are essential to understanding the theoretical foundation of ENDOCRED, credibility-driven forecasting, and risk-based monetary policy.

  • Laxton, D., Galstyan, M., & Avagyan, V. (2024) Prudent Risk Management Approach to Monetary Policy Central Bank of Armenia, forthcoming. Download here

  • Adrian, T., Laxton, D., & Obstfeld, M. (Eds.) (2018) Frontiers of Monetary Policy Making International Monetary Fund. Download here

  • Kostanyan, A., Matinyan, A., & Papikyan, A. (2022) Getting FIT with Imperfect Policy Credibility DYNARE/JULIA Workshops with an Application for the US Economy (2022) Central Bank of Armenia. Download here

  • Kostanyan, A., Matinyan, A., & Papikyan, A. (2022) Getting FIT with Imperfect Policy Credibility: DYNARE/JULIA Workshops with an Application for a Small Open Economy Central Bank of Armenia. Download here

  • Laxton, D., Igityan, H., & Mkhatrishvili, S. (2024) Adapting Monetary Policy Frameworks for Risk Management Forthcoming in the Oxford Review of Economic Policy. Download here

  • Clinton, K., Hlédik, T., Holub, T., Laxton, D., & Wang, H. (2017) Czech Magic: Implementing Inflation-Forecast Targeting at the CNB IMF Working Paper 17/21. Download here

  • Laxton, D., Freedman, C., Kumhof, M., Laxton, J., Muir, D., Mursula, S., & Wang, H. (2015) Adding the Exchange Rate as a Tool to Combat Deflationary Risks in the Czech Republic IMF Working Paper 15/74. Download here

  • Greenspan, A. (2003) Monetary Policy under Uncertainty Jackson Hole Symposium Speech. Download here

  • Bernanke, B. (2024) Forecasting for Monetary Policy Making and Communication: A Review Bank of England. Download here

Further Reading & Transparency Reports - The Better Policy Project – Research Papers - Central Bank of Armenia – Transparency Report Q4 2024

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)
docs.text:
Who should I contact for questions, collaboration, or training? For any questions about using DynareJulia, contributing to the project, or organizing training sessions and workshops, please contact: Asya Kostanyan – asyakostanyan@thebetterpolicyproject.org Is training available for DynareJulia? Yes! We offer ongoing training opportunities through The Better Policy Project, including:
  • Introductory courses on DynareJulia and macroeconomic modeling
  • Hands-on workshops on scenario analysis, ENDOCRED, and DSGE models
  • Custom trainings for central banks and research teams
To schedule a session, contact asyakostanyan@thebetterpolicyproject.org. What is DynareJulia? DynareJulia is an open-source modeling engine written in Julia that builds on the well-established Dynare for MATLAB/Octave framework. It supports solving, simulating, and analyzing macroeconomic models written in .mod files — including support for perfect foresight, stochastic simulations, and modular frameworks like ENDOCRED. How is this different from Dynare (MATLAB version)? DynareJulia reimplements many of the core features of Dynare in pure Julia, which offers:
  • Faster performance
  • Better modularity and flexibility
  • Full integration with Julia packages like Plots.jl and DataFrames.jl
  • No need for MATLAB licenses
Some advanced Dynare features are still being implemented. Is this only for advanced users? No! DynareJulia is suitable for:
  • Beginners learning DSGE modeling
  • Central banks using FPAS frameworks
  • Advanced researchers working with nonlinear, modular, or multi-country models
Is DynareJulia free? Yes. DynareJulia is 100% free and open source — and it will remain that way. You can use it, modify it, and redistribute it under a permissive license. What is PythonDynareJulia? PythonDynareJulia is an upcoming project under development that provides a Python-based frontend for DynareJulia. It will allow users to:
  • Run and simulate .mod files directly from Python
  • Use Jupyter notebooks or GUI-like interfaces
  • Visualize results using Python libraries like matplotlib or pandas
More details coming soon. What features are currently supported?
  • Solving DSGE and perfect foresight models
  • Using the @dynare macro or Dynare.run_model()
  • Visualizing impulse response functions
  • Running multiple model scenarios
  • Integrating with Julia scripting for advanced workflows
Is this a final release? No. DynareJulia is actively under development. New features like estimation, nonlinear solvers, and advanced diagnostics are in progress. Can I contribute? Yes! We welcome contributions:
  • Share your models and use cases
  • Improve documentation and tutorials
  • Report issues or suggest features on GitHub
  • Help expand functionality
Where can I learn more? Explore these pages:
  • Video Tutorials
  • Simple Model Walkthrough
  • ENDOCRED Use Case
  • Research Papers
Also check out:
  • DynareJulia GitHub Repository
  • DynareJulia Documentation

For anything else, reach out to asyakostanyan@thebetterpolicyproject.org

docs.title: Getting Started with DynareJulia
docs.text: This section will walk you through installing Julia — the foundational language for running DynareJulia.

docs.title: Step 1: Download Julia
docs.text:
DynareJulia currently requires Julia version 1.9.4 for compatibility and performance reasons.

🛠️ You must install version 1.9.4, not the latest version.

To download it:

👉 Visit the Julia Old Releases Page

Then scroll down to find: Version 1.9.4 (released on 2023-12-21)

Select the appropriate installer based on your operating system:

docs.title: 💻 For Windows
docs.text:
• Click on: julia-1.9.4-win64.exe (or julia-1.9.4-win32.exe if using 32-bit)
  • Run the installer
  • Accept default settings (you can check “Add Julia to PATH” if available)

docs.title: 🍎 For macOS
docs.text:
• Click on: julia-1.9.4-mac64.dmg
  • Open the .dmg file and drag Julia into your Applications folder

docs.title: 🐧 For Linux
docs.text:
• Download the julia-1.9.4-linux-x86_64.tar.gz
  • Extract it using your terminal

docs.title: Step 2: Testing
docs.text:
Step 3: Install Visual Studio Code (VS Code)

Visual Studio Code (VS Code) is a lightweight and powerful code editor. We recommend it for writing and managing Julia code, editing .mod model files, and building this documentation.

docs.title: 📥 Download VS Code
docs.text:
1. Go to the official VS Code download page
  2. Choose your operating system:
  3. Windows: Click on the .exe installer
  4. macOS: Choose the .zip or .dmg version
  5. Linux: Download the .deb or .rpm file

docs.title: 💻 Windows
docs.text:
• Run the downloaded .exe file
  • During setup:
  • ✅ Enable “Add to PATH”
  • ✅ Enable “Open with Code” in the right-click context menu
  • Complete the installation by clicking Next through the installer

docs.title: 🍎 macOS
docs.text:
• Open the downloaded .dmg file
  • Drag the Visual Studio Code icon into your Applications folder
  • Open VS Code from the Applications menu or using Spotlight (Cmd + Space, then type "Code")

docs.title: Step 4: Install the Julia Extension in VS Code
docs.text: To enable full Julia support in VS Code — including syntax highlighting, interactive REPL, inline plots, variable browser, and more — you need to install the official Julia extension.

docs.title: 4.1 How to Install the Julia Extension
docs.text:
1. Open Visual Studio Code
  2. Click on the Extensions icon in the left sidebar (or press Ctrl+Shift+X)

  3. In the search bar at the top, type: Julia

  4. Look for the extension published by Julia Language — it should appear first in the list

  5. Name: Julia

  6. Publisher: Julia Language

  7. Click the Install button

docs.title: 4.2 First-Time Setup
docs.text:
After installing the extension:

  1. Open a Julia file, or click View → Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P)
  2. Type: Execute Active File
  3. VS Code will prompt you to select the Julia executable path (if it doesn't auto-detect it)

  4. Browse to the path where Julia 1.9.4 is installed:

  5. On Windows (default):

C:\Users\<YourUsername>\AppData\Local\Programs\Julia-1.9.4\bin\julia.exe

  • On macOS:

/Applications/Julia-1.9.app/Contents/Resources/julia/bin/julia

  • On Linux (if installed via tarball):

/opt/julia-1.9.4/bin/julia

  1. Select the correct path → the extension will configure automatically

docs.title: ✅ Verify Julia REPL Integration
docs.text:
To check if it's working:

  1. Create a new file and save it as test.jl
  2. Add:

println("Hello from Julia in VS Code!")

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This section explains how to install the necessary components to use DynareJulia, starting with setting up a Python virtual environment and preparing to install Dynare.

docs.title: Step 1: Set Up a Virtual Environment
docs.text: Before installing Dynare, it's recommended to create a virtual environment (venv) inside the folder where your model files will be stored.

docs.title: What is a Virtual Environment?
docs.text:
A virtual environment is an isolated workspace that keeps your project's dependencies (such as Python packages or Dynare wrappers) separate from your system-wide configuration.

Using a venv helps: - Keep each modeling project self-contained - Avoid conflicts with other tools or versions - Improve reproducibility when sharing your setup

docs.title: 1.1 Create a project folder
docs.text: Create a directory where your .mod files and Julia scripts will live: activate venv

docs.title: Step 2: Install Dynare in Julia
docs.text: Once your virtual environment is ready and Julia is installed, the next step is to install the Dynare.jl package using Julia’s package manager.

docs.title: 2.1 Open the Julia REPL
docs.text: There are two ways to launch the Julia REPL:

docs.title: Option A: From your system terminal
docs.text:
Simply type:

julia

docs.title: Option B: From inside Visual Studio Code
docs.text:
1. Press Ctrl + Shift + P (or Cmd + Shift + P on macOS)
  2. In the Command Palette, type:
  3. Press Enter VS Code will open a terminal panel with the Julia REPL running.

docs.title: 2.2 Add the Dynare.jl package
docs.text:
Once the REPL is open, enter the following commands:

using Pkg
Pkg.add("Dynare")

docs.title: 2.3 First-Time Use
docs.text:
After installing Dynare.jl, test that the package loads correctly.

In the Julia REPL, type:

using Dynare

Note: The first time you use the package, Julia will precompile it. This may take a minute.

Recommendation: After installing packages, it is good practice to restart your Julia REPL or VS Code terminal. This ensures that all changes take effect properly, especially when adding new packages or changing environments.

docs.title: 2.4 Optional: Check the Installed Version
docs.text:
To verify which version of Dynare was installed, run:

Pkg.status("Dynare")

You should see an output like:

Dynare v0.9.18

This confirms the package is installed and active in your Julia environment.

docs.title: 2.5 Install Required Julia Packages for Modeling
docs.text:
In addition to Dynare, you will need several additional Julia packages for typical modeling workflows:

  • PATHSolver — used for solving models with complementarity conditions
  • DataFrames — for structured data manipulation
  • Dates — to handle time-based elements like periods or ranges
  • Plots — for generating charts and visualizations

docs.title: Step 1: Launch Julia
docs.text:
In your terminal, start Julia by typing:

julia

docs.title: Step 2: Install the required packages
docs.text:
Once the Julia REPL opens, run the following commands:

using Pkg
Pkg.add([
    "PATHSolver",
    "DataFrames",
    "Dates",
    "Plots"
])

docs.title: Step 3: Test the installation
docs.text:
To verify that everything is working, enter:

using PATHSolver, DataFrames, Dates, Plots

docs.title: 2.6 Configure the PATH License
docs.text:
Dynare uses the PATH solver by S. Dirkse, M.C. Ferris, and T. Munson — as accessed through PATHSolver.jl — to solve perfect foresight models with occasionally binding constraints.

To use it, you need to register the free license key in your Julia startup file.

docs.title: 1. Obtain the license
docs.text:
The license is publicly available at:

https://pages.cs.wisc.edu/~ferris/path/LICENSE

For convenience, here is the license string: setenv PATH_LICENSE_STRING "2830898829&Courtesy&&&USR&45321&5_1_2021&1000&PATH&GEN&31_12_2025&0_0_0&6000&0_0"

docs.title: 2. Add the license to your startup.jl file
docs.text:
Create a file named startup.jl and add the following line:

ENV["PATH_LICENSE_STRING"] = "2830898829&Courtesy&&&USR&45321&5_1_2021&1000&PATH&GEN&31_12_2025&0_0_0&6000&0_0"

docs.title: 💻 Windows (File Explorer method)
docs.text:
1. Open File Explorer and go to your user folder (e.g., C:\Users\YourName).
  2. Navigate to the .julia folder and create a new folder named config if it doesn't already exist.
  3. Inside .julia\config, create a new text file:
  4. Right-click → New → Text Document
  5. Open the new file and paste the following line:
ENV["PATH_LICENSE_STRING"] = "2830898829&Courtesy&&&USR&45321&5_1_2021&1000&PATH&GEN&31_12_2025&0_0_0&6000&0_0"
  1. Save the file:
  2. Go to File → Save As
  3. In Save as type, choose All Files
  4. In File name, type: startup.jl
  5. Click Save

docs.title: 🍎 macOS / Linux (terminal method)
docs.text:
1. Open a terminal.
  2. Create the Julia config folder if it doesn't exist: bash mkdir -p ~/.julia/config
  3. Open the startup.jl file in your preferred text editor (e.g., nano, vim, or VS Code): bash nano ~/.julia/config/startup.jl
  4. Paste this line into the file: julia ENV["PATH_LICENSE_STRING"] = "2830898829&Courtesy&&&USR&45321&5_1_2021&1000&PATH&GEN&31_12_2025&0_0_0&6000&0_0"
  5. Save and close the file.

docs.title: Potential Errors
docs.text:
Common errors you may run into when setting up DynareJulia on Windows, and how to fix them. Click any error message below to expand the diagnosis and fix.

UndefVarError: GeneralizedFirstOrderAlgorithm not defined **You see something like this when loading Dynare:** 
ERROR: LoadError: UndefVarError: `GeneralizedFirstOrderAlgorithm` not defined
   @ C:\Users\<you>\.julia\packages\Dynare\<hash>\src\global\types.jl:150
in expression starting at .../Dynare.jl:1
ERROR: Failed to precompile Dynare ...
**Why it happens.** Dynare 0.10.0 referenced a symbol (GeneralizedFirstOrderAlgorithm) that the NonlinearSolve package later renamed. If your Manifest.toml pins Dynare 0.10.0 but Pkg resolves the rest of the dependency tree against the newest NonlinearSolve, the symbol is gone and Dynare fails to precompile. This usually happens when a Manifest.toml was generated against an older Julia version (e.g. 1.9) and is now being used under Julia 1.10 or later. **Fix.** Wipe the lockfile and let Pkg re-resolve from the Project.toml. This pulls Dynare 0.10.4 (or whatever the latest compatible version is) with a matching set of dependencies. In a PowerShell terminal at the project root: 
# 1. Activate the project (sets JULIA_PROJECT to .\venv)
. .\activate.ps1

# 2. Delete the lockfile
Remove-Item .\venv\Manifest.toml

# 3. Re-resolve and precompile
julia -e "using Pkg; Pkg.instantiate(); Pkg.precompile(); Pkg.status()"
After this finishes, Pkg.status() should report: 
[a93c6f00] DataFrames v1.8.2
[5203de40] Dynare v0.10.4    <-- was 0.10.0 before the fix
[91a5bcdd] Plots v1.41.6
[bd369af6] Tables v1.12.1
If Dynare is still listed as 0.10.0, your Project.toml has a hard pin — remove the [compat] Dynare line and try again. cannot remove ... Access is denied [stockflow/model/json] **You see this when running a model:** 
Starting preprocessing of the model file ...
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::filesystem::__cxx11::filesystem_error'
  what():  filesystem error: cannot remove: Access is denied [stockflow/model/json]
ERROR: LoadError: type Array has no field results
**Why it happens.** Two things together:
  • The Dynare preprocessor wants to delete the output folder from a previous run before writing fresh output.
  • Dropbox or OneDrive holds file locks on those files while syncing them. The "Access is denied" is the sync client refusing to let go.
When the preprocessor crashes, @dynare returns an empty Vector{Any} instead of a Context — that's why the next line errors with "type Array has no field results". **Fix.** Two options, in order of preference: Move the project out of Dropbox. Put your Codes\ folder somewhere like C:\dev\thailand2026\. Keep slides and PDFs in Dropbox; keep the code out. DSGE solver output churns hundreds of small files per run, and Dropbox sync hates that. Or pause Dropbox before running. Right-click the Dropbox tray icon → Pause syncing → run your model → resume. If you control the driver script, you can also add a safe_rm() helper that retries with backoff — it papers over transient locks but not persistent ones. Failed to precompile GR_jll / Plots / Dynare **You see a cascade of precompile failures:** 
Precompiling project...
  ✗ GR_jll
  ✗ Qt6ShaderTools_jll
  ✗ GR
  ✗ Plots
  ✗ StatsPlots
  ✗ Dynare
  N dependencies errored.
Notice the cascade: GR_jll is the root failure; everything else fails because it depends on Plots which depends on GR. **Why it happens.** Almost always Windows long-path limits. The default MAX_PATH is 260 characters. A depot path like: 
C:\Users\asyak\Dropbox\Work\The Better Policy Project\Courses\Thailand 2026\DSGE - Week 2\Codes\.julia\artifacts\<40-char-hash>\bin\gr\fonts\...
is already 165 characters before GR's nested folder structure starts. GR routinely overflows it. **Fix.** Use a short depot path that lives outside Dropbox.
  1. Edit activate.ps1 to set:
$env:JULIA_DEPOT_PATH = "C:\jl-depots\thailand2026"
  1. Re-activate and re-instantiate:
. .\activate.ps1
julia -e "using Pkg; Pkg.instantiate(); Pkg.precompile()"
The Project.toml and Manifest.toml stay in venv\ (small text files, fine in Dropbox). Only the multi-GB depot moves to a short path on the system drive. Alternative: enable Windows long-path support globally. Open gpedit.msc → Computer Configuration → Administrative Templates → System → Filesystem → "Enable Win32 long paths" → Enabled. Requires admin and a reboot. ArgumentError: invalid index ! when extracting IRFs **You see one of these when plotting impulse responses:** 
ERROR: ArgumentError: invalid index: ! of type typeof(!)
or: 
MethodError: no method matching expand_extrema!(::Plots.Axis,
  ::AxisArrayTables.AxisArrayTable{Float64, ...})
**Why it happens.** DynareJulia returns IRFs as AxisArrayTable, not DataFrame. DataFrame indexing syntax (tbl[!, :col] or tbl[:, :col]) does not work — ! is a function, and : returns a one-column 2D table that Plots can't accept as a y-series. **Fix.** Use the Tables.jl column accessor and convert to a plain vector: 
using Tables
series = collect(Float64, Tables.getcolumn(irfs[shock], :b))
This works whether the underlying type is DataFrame, AxisArrayTable, or anything else implementing the Tables.jl interface — so the script stays robust across Dynare versions. activate.ps1 : The term '.\activate.ps1' is not recognized **You see this in PowerShell:** 
activate.ps1 : The term 'activate.ps1' is not recognized as the name of a
cmdlet, function, script file, or operable program.
**Why it happens.** PowerShell will not run a script in the current folder unless you prefix it with .\. And to keep the environment variables in your shell (rather than throwing them away in a child process), you also need to dot-source the script with a leading .  (period + space). **Fix.** Run it with both prefixes: 
. .\activate.ps1
That is: period — space — period — backslash — activate.ps1. Four characters before the filename. If you also see "running scripts is disabled on this system", run this once per machine: 
Set-ExecutionPolicy -Scope CurrentUser -ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned

docs.title: Perfect-foresight simulation (Dynare.jl 0.10.x)
docs.text:
A few rough edges in the perfect-foresight subsystem of Dynare.jl 0.10.x. These bite if you port a model from MATLAB Dynare or follow older tutorials.

perfect_foresight_setup / _solver run silently and produce no simulation **You see this:** the model parses, the steady state computes correctly, no obvious error is thrown — but `context.results.model_results[1].simulations` is an **empty** Vector{Simulation}, and any attempt to read `simulations[1]` blows up with BoundsError. Earlier in the log, hidden in the parser output, you may also spot a stray: 
MethodError(convert, (Float64, nothing), 0x...)
**Why it happens.** The legacy MATLAB-Dynare two-step pattern in the `.mod` file — 
perfect_foresight_setup(periods = 200);
perfect_foresight_solver;
— is not parsed correctly in Dynare.jl 0.10.x. The keyword option `(periods = 200)` round-trips through a code path that produces `nothing` where a `Float64` is expected, the parser swallows the `MethodError`, and nothing populates the simulations vector. **Fix.** Use the modern single statement, with the trailing !: 
perfect_foresight!(periods = 200);
Drop both `perfect_foresight_setup` and `perfect_foresight_solver` from the `.mod` file. Same applies if you call from Julia — use Dynare.perfect_foresight!(; context, periods = 200), not Dynare.perfect_foresight_setup! + Dynare.perfect_foresight_solver!. UndefVarError: guess_values not defined when using linearinterpolation **You see this when calling perfect_foresight! with a non-default initialization:** 
ERROR: LoadError: UndefVarError: `guess_values` not defined
Stacktrace:
 [1] perfect_foresight_initialization!(...)
   @ Dynare .../perfectforesight/perfectforesight.jl:504
**Why it happens.** `Dynare.linearinterpolation` is declared as a value of the InitializationAlgo enum, but the corresponding branch in perfect_foresight_initialization! is empty — the local variable guess_values never gets assigned. Same story for Dynare.firstorder: it dispatches to simul_first_order!, which calls compute_first_order_solution! with the wrong number of arguments and crashes with MethodError. **Fix.** Of the four enum values — initvalfile, steadystate, firstorder, linearinterpolation — only steadystate (the default) actually works in 0.10.x. Don't pass initialization_algo at all, or pass Dynare.steadystate explicitly. If your problem is a long transition where the steady-state initial guess is too far from the true path, you can usually rescue convergence by setting the initial state with a histval block instead of trying to interpolate by hand: 
initval;
    K = K_SS;       // guess for steady state, refined by `steady;` below
    ...
end;

steady;

histval;
    K(0) = K_SS/2;  // initial state: K(-1) at half the steady-state level
end;

perfect_foresight!(periods = 200);
This pattern is also what Dynare.jl's own test models use (see test/models/initialization/neoclassical1.mod in the package). MethodError: Cannot convert an object of type Missing to an object of type Float64 (when plotting) **You see this when extracting a simulation column for plotting:** 
ERROR: LoadError: MethodError: Cannot `convert` an object of type Missing
       to an object of type Float64
Stacktrace:
 [1] setindex!(A::Vector{Float64}, x::Missing, i1::Int64)
 [2] copyto_unaliased!(...)
 ...
 [5] collect(::Type{Float64}, itr::AxisArrays.AxisVector{Union{Missing, Float64}, ...})
**Why it happens.** Perfect-foresight simulation tables in Dynare.jl come back typed as Union{Missing, Float64}: the boundary rows (period 0 and the period after the simulation horizon) can hold missing values for variables that aren't pinned at those points. collect(Float64, ...) can't convert missing, so it errors out. **Fix.** Replace missing with NaN before passing the column to Plots — Plots renders NaN as a gap in the line: 
raw    = Tables.getcolumn(sim, :K)
series = [ismissing(x) ? NaN : Float64(x) for x in raw]

plot(0:length(series)-1, series; lw = 2, label = "K")
Use the same pattern (raw → coalesce-to-NaN → plot) for every variable you read out of a simulation table.

docs.title: Working setup reference
docs.text:
The two-command workflow once everything is in place:

cd "C:\...\Codes"
. .\activate.ps1
julia run_stockflow.jl

activate.ps1 sets JULIA_PROJECT to .\venv (and, if you applied the long-path fix above, JULIA_DEPOT_PATH to a short path).

A successful run ends with output like:

================ Steady state ================
  b        =   0.500000
  def      =   0.014563
  prdef    =  -0.004854
  intpay   =   0.019417
  y        =   0.000000
==============================================
IRF chart saved to stockflow_irfs.png

For anything not covered here, reach out to asyakostanyan@thebetterpolicyproject.org.

docs.title: Simple Model
docs.text: This page demonstrates how to run a simple linear macroeconomic model in DynareJulia using a .mod file and a Julia driver script. The model is a small, FPAS-style gap model with an endogenous credibility block, solved under perfect foresight.

docs.title: Download the Files
docs.text:
Download both files and place them in the same project directory:

  • simple_linear.mod — the model file
  • simple_linear.jl — the Julia script that runs the model and plots the results

docs.title: 📄 Dynare Model File (simple_linear.mod)
docs.text:
var 
PIE             // QoQ Inflation 
PIE4            // 1 year moving average of inflation  
PIE_EXP         // Inflation Expectations
C               // Credibility 
SIGNAL_CBP      // CB Credibility Signal
RES_PIE4_LOW    // 
RES_PIE4_HIGH   // 
Y               // Output gap
RS              // Nominal interest rate
RR              // Real interest rate
RR_BAR          // Real interest rate trend
RR_GAP          // Real interest rate gap
UNR             // Unemployment rate
UNR_GAP         // Unemployment GAP
UNR_BAR         // Equilibrium Unemployment Rate (NAIRU)
g_UNR           // Persistent component in NAIRU
DL_GDP          // Real GDP, Q-o-Q Log Growth
DL_GDP_BAR      // Potential GDP, Q-o-Q Log Growth
G_GDP_BAR       // Persistent Component in Potential GDP Growth
;


varexo 
E_Y             // Output Gap shock
E_PIE           // Inflation Shock
E_DL_GDP_BAR    // Potential GDP Shock
E_G_GDP_BAR     // Persistent Shock of Potential GDP Growth
E_UNR_BAR       // NAIRU Shock 
E_UNR_g         // Persistent Shock of NAIRU 
E_UNR           // Unemployment Gap Shock 
E_RS            // Interest Rate Shock
E_RR_BAR        // Real Interest Rate Trend Shock
E_PIE_EXP       // Shock to Inflation Expectations 
E_C             // Credibility Shock 
;

parameters BETA1, BETA2, BETA3, LAMBDA1, LAMBDA2, GROWTH_SS, ALPHA1, ALPHA2, ALPHA3, ALPHA4,
           ALPHA5, ALPHA6, ALPHA7, ALPHA8, PIE_SS, UNR_SS, RR_BAR_STAR, GAMMA1, GAMMA2, GAMMA4;

BETA1 = 0.75;
BETA2 = 0.15;
BETA3 = 0.20;
LAMBDA1 = 0.50;
LAMBDA2 = 0.25;
GROWTH_SS = 2.50;
ALPHA1 = 0.90;
ALPHA2 = 0.90;
ALPHA3 = 0.90;
ALPHA4 = 0.50;
ALPHA5 = 0.80;
ALPHA6 = 0.30;
ALPHA7 = 0.15;
ALPHA8 = 0.90;
PIE_SS = 2.0;
UNR_SS = 4.50;
RR_BAR_STAR = 0.50;
GAMMA1 = 0.50;
GAMMA2 = 1.50;
GAMMA4 = 0.20;



model;

Y = BETA1*Y(-1) + BETA2*Y(+1) - BETA3*RR_GAP(-1) + E_Y;

PIE = LAMBDA1*PIE_EXP + (1 - LAMBDA1)*PIE4(-1) + LAMBDA2*Y(-1) + E_PIE;

PIE_EXP = C*PIE4(+4) + (1 - C)*PIE4(-1) + 0.1*(1 - C) + E_PIE_EXP; 

C = 0.04*SIGNAL_CBP + (1-0.04)*C(-1) + E_C;

SIGNAL_CBP =  RES_PIE4_HIGH^2/(RES_PIE4_HIGH^2+RES_PIE4_LOW^2) ; 

// RES_PIE4_LOW =    Actual           -          Forecast
RES_PIE4_LOW =       PIE4    -      (   0.5*PIE_SS+0.5*PIE4(-1))   ;

// RES_PIE4_HIGH =    Actual           -          Forecast
RES_PIE4_HIGH =       PIE4    -      (   0.1*10 + 0.9*PIE4(-1))   ;

PIE4 = 0.25*(PIE+PIE(-1)+PIE(-2)+PIE(-3));

DL_GDP = DL_GDP_BAR + (Y - Y(-1));

DL_GDP_BAR = ALPHA1*DL_GDP_BAR(-1) + (1-ALPHA1)*G_GDP_BAR + E_DL_GDP_BAR;

G_GDP_BAR = ALPHA2*G_GDP_BAR(-1) + (1-ALPHA2)*GROWTH_SS + E_G_GDP_BAR;

RR = RS-PIE(+1);

RR_BAR = ALPHA3*RR_BAR(-1) + (1-ALPHA3)*RR_BAR_STAR + E_RR_BAR;

RR_GAP = RR - RR_BAR;

UNR_GAP = UNR_BAR - UNR;

UNR_BAR = ALPHA8*UNR_BAR(-1) + (1-ALPHA8)*UNR_SS + g_UNR + E_UNR_BAR;

g_UNR = (1-ALPHA4)*g_UNR(-1) + E_UNR_g;

UNR_GAP = ALPHA5*UNR_GAP(-1) + ALPHA6*Y + ALPHA7*Y(+1) + E_UNR;

RS = GAMMA1*RS(-1) + (1 - GAMMA1)*(RR_BAR + PIE4(+3) + GAMMA2*(PIE4(+3) - PIE_SS) + GAMMA4*Y) + E_RS;

end;



steady_state_model;
PIE = PIE_SS;
PIE4 = PIE_SS;
Y = 0;
G_GDP_BAR = GROWTH_SS;
DL_GDP_BAR = GROWTH_SS;
DL_GDP = GROWTH_SS;
RR_BAR = RR_BAR_STAR;
RR = RR_BAR_STAR;
RR_GAP = 0;
RS = RR + PIE;
UNR = UNR_SS;
UNR_GAP = 0;
UNR_BAR = UNR_SS;
g_UNR = 0;
SIGNAL_CBP = 1;
C = 1;
PIE_EXP = PIE_SS;
RES_PIE4_LOW = 0;
RES_PIE4_HIGH = -0.8;
end;



steady;

histval;
C(0) = 0.95;
Y(0) = 1.0;
RS(0) = 4.3; // Average Effective Fed Funds Rate 2025Q1
PIE(0) = 3.0; //2.1;  // Nowcast for Core PCE 2025Q1, QoQ Annualized, April 29, 2025
UNR_BAR(0)  = 4.5;
UNR(0)  = 4.1; //2025Q1
UNR_GAP(0) = 0.4;
end;


scenario!(name = :E_PIE, period = 1, value = 1.0);
scenario!(name = :E_PIE, period = 2, value = 0.8);
scenario!(name = :E_PIE, period = 3, value = 0.5);
scenario!(name = :E_Y, period = 1, value = -1.0);

// simul(periods=80);
perfect_foresight_setup(periods=80);
perfect_foresight_solver(lmmcp);

Description:

  • The model combines an output-gap (IS) equation, a Phillips curve, an endogenous credibility block (C, SIGNAL_CBP, PIE_EXP), unemployment/NAIRU dynamics, potential-GDP growth, and a forward-looking policy rule for the nominal rate RS.
  • A steady_state_model block provides the analytical steady state, and histval sets the initial conditions (2025Q1).
  • The scenario! statements impose a sequence of inflation (E_PIE) and output-gap (E_Y) shocks over the first periods.
  • The model is solved under perfect foresight with the lmmcp (mixed complementarity) solver via perfect_foresight_setup / perfect_foresight_solver.

docs.title: ▶️ Running the Model in Julia (simple_linear.jl)
docs.text:
The driver script below activates a project environment, runs the model with the @dynare macro, extracts the simulation into a DataFrame, and plots key variables (RS, Y, PIE, UNR) on a quarterly time axis.

using Pkg
Pkg.activate("venv")

using Dynare
using PATHSolver
using DataFrames
using Dates
using Plots

# --- Set backend ---
gr()

# --- Step 1: Define Model Files ---
model_files = [
    "simple_linear.mod",
]

# --- Step 2: Run Dynare Models ---
results = Dict{String, Any}()
for model in model_files
    if isfile(model)
        println("Running model: ", model)
        results[model] = eval(Meta.parse("@dynare \"$model\""))
        println("Finished: ", model, "\n")
    else
        println("Model file not found: ", model)
    end
end

# --- Step 3: Extract Data into DataFrames ---
df_models = Dict()
for model in keys(results)
    df_models[model] = DataFrame(results[model].results.model_results[1].simulations[1].data)
end

# --- Step 4: Generate Quarterly Time Index (2025Q1–2035Q2) ---
time_index = (Date(2025,2):Quarter(1):Date(2035,3))[1:40]
formatted_labels = [string(year(d)) * "-Q" * string(month(d) ÷ 3 + 1) for d in time_index]

# --- Step 5: Plot Variables and Colors ---
variables_to_plot = [:RS, :Y, :PIE, :UNR]
model_colors = Dict(
    "simple_linear.mod" => :blue
)

# --- Step 6: Create 2x2 Grid Plot (No Legends) ---
plot_grid = plot(layout=(2,2), size=(900,700), legend=false)

for (i, var) in enumerate(variables_to_plot)
    for (model, df) in df_models
        if haskey(model_colors, model)
            y = df[!, var][1:40]  # Use the full 40 quarters, starting from 2025Q1
            plot!(1:40, y,
                  label="", lw=2, color=model_colors[model],
                  subplot=i, xrotation=90)
        end
    end
    title!(subplot=i, string(var))
    xticks!(subplot=i, 1:4:40, formatted_labels[1:4:40])
end

# --- Step 7: Save and Display ---
savefig(plot_grid, "simple_linear.png")
display(plot_grid)

This will:

  • Activate the venv project environment and load the required packages.
  • Run simple_linear.mod through the @dynare macro, which parses, solves, and simulates the model.
  • Collect the simulated paths into a DataFrame.
  • Produce a 2×2 grid plot of the nominal interest rate (RS), output gap (Y), inflation (PIE), and unemployment rate (UNR), saved as simple_linear.png.

!!! note Make sure simple_linear.mod and simple_linear.jl are in the same directory, and run the script from that directory so the model file is found.
